---
title: "Expanding Your Vocabulary: A Framework for Topic Integration in Texts"
date: 2026-04-27
author:
  - name: Roy Gardner
    url: https://github.com/roybgardner
    affiliation: Comparative Constitutions Project, University of Texas at Austin
  - name: Matthew Martin
    url: https://mattjamesmartin.github.io/
    orcid: 0000-0003-3460-3603
    affiliation: Department of Government, University of Texas at Austin
  - name: Ashley Moran
    url: https://liberalarts.utexas.edu/government/faculty/am49396
    orcid: 0000-0001-5969-2134
    affiliation: Department of Government, University of Texas at Austin
  - name: Zachary Elkins
    url: https://sites.google.com/site/zachelkinstexas/
    orcid: 0000-0002-7932-773X
    affiliation: Department of Government, University of Texas at Austin
  - name: Andrés Cruz
    url: https://arcruz0.github.io/
    orcid: 0000-0003-3819-9433
    affiliation: Department of Government, University of Texas at Austin
  - name: Guillermo Pérez
    url: https://liberalarts.utexas.edu/government/gradstudents/gp22823
    orcid: 0009-0007-1513-2013
    affiliation: Department of Government, University of Texas at Austin
categories: 
  - Topic integration
  - Segments-as-topic
  - Vocabularies
  - Semantic similarity
  - Constitutions
pub-info:
  year: "2026"
  venue: "Social Science Computer Review"
  blurb: >-
    Topic discovery and integration are vital for maintaining vocabularies that categorize textual corpora. Automated approaches are often computationally expensive and lack domain-specific conceptual nuance; manual approaches are costly in terms of time and potential bias. We introduce the segments-as-topic (SAT) methodology, a four-stage process that combines automation and human expertise to assess candidate topics for vocabulary inclusion. We apply this methodology to the Comparative Constitutions Project vocabulary that tracks over 330 topics in national constitutions, and demonstrate the addition of three new topics. The SAT approach balances computational efficiency with expert judgment, offering a systematic, user-friendly, and replicable framework for social scientists to expand domain-specific vocabularies.
  links:
  - name: Final version
    url: https://doi.org/10.1177/08944393261443513
    icon: fa-solid fa-arrow-up-right-from-square
  - name: GitHub
    url: https://github.com/conceptintegration/Segments-as-Topics
    icon: fa-brands fa-github
  - name: Tutorial
    url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sm_0M5XHkk0
    icon: fa-brands fa-youtube
doi: 10.1177/08944393261443513
---

## Abstract

Topic discovery and integration are vital for maintaining vocabularies that categorize textual corpora. Automated approaches are often computationally expensive and lack domain-specific conceptual nuance; manual approaches are costly in terms of time and potential bias. To address this dilemma, we introduce the segments-as-topic (SAT) methodology, a four-stage process that combines automation and human expertise to assess candidate topics for vocabulary inclusion. In the SAT generation stage, a topic is formulated and refined through collaboration with domain experts, then a sentence-level semantic similarity model retrieves corpus segments semantically aligned with the topic. The SAT expansion stage uses this seed set to find additional semantically similar segments, which are iteratively accepted or rejected to build a final segment set. During the review stage, a panel of scholars evaluates the topic for inclusion. In the integration stage, all segments in the final segment set are automatically tagged with the new topic. We apply this methodology to the Comparative Constitutions Project vocabulary that tracks over 330 topics in national constitutions, and demonstrate the addition of three new topics to the vocabulary. The SAT approach balances computational efficiency with expert judgment, offering a systematic, user-friendly, and replicable framework for social scientists to expand domain-specific vocabularies.{{< fa blank >}}
